summary: Uncle Bob responds to DHH's post about testing practices, offering point-by-point agreement and disagreement with DHH's seven testing guidelines. He pushes back on DHH's recommendation against 100% coverage, arguing it should remain an asymptotic goal, and disagrees with DHH's code-to-test ratios, preferring 1:1. He agrees that testing frameworks is usually unnecessary, that integration tests should be reserved for integration issues, and that obviously trivial code may not need tests. The post ends with a correction noting DHH's TSA post actually predated his 'TDD is Dead' post.
key_points: 100% test coverage should be treated as an asymptotic goal - no other number makes a reasonable target
Code-to-test ratio should be approximately 1:1 (20K lines of code = 20K lines of tests)
Writing tests with TDD requires 'negative time' - it saves time rather than consuming it
Don't test your framework unless you have reason not to trust it
Keep tests focused and close to the code - avoid testing through UIs or web servers
money_quotes: Aim as high as you can. Treat 100% as an asymptotic goal. No other number makes a reasonable goal.
Testing should take none of your time. Writing tests, with TDD, requires negative time (i.e. it saves you a boatload).
Every worthwhile test you don't write costs you time.
There's generally no reason to test your framework; so long as you trust it.
Keep your tests focused. Don't test through UIs. Don't test through web servers. Test as close to the code as you can.
Be careful. Sometimes one line of code has 20 lines of semantics.
key_insight: Writing tests with TDD doesn't cost time - it saves time, and every worthwhile test you skip will ultimately cost you more than writing it would have.
